coredata.dc:title: A facile method to prepare Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite as advanced anode material for lithium ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Feng
Liu, Quanrun
Jin, Zhanzhan
Huang, Guangxu
Xing, Baolin
Jia, Jianbo
Zhang, Chuanxiang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hybrid compositions have aroused wide attention with regard to their application in energy storage; however, complex procedures for their separation, purification and functionalization hinder their practical application. The present study put forward a facile and environmentally friendly coprecipitation approach to prepare Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites using coal tar pitch (CTP) as “natural GQDs” for the first time. When used as anode in lithium ion batteries (LIBs), the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite demonstrates high reversible capacity of 810 mAh g−1 over 200 cycles at the current density of 100 mA g−1. Moreover, at a high current density of 2000 mA g−1, the reversible capacity reaches 547 mAh g−1, which is 6 times that of pure Fe3O4 (91 mAh g−1). The marked ameliorated electrochemical performance is attributed to the fact that the successful coupling between Fe3O4 and CTP QDs can not only improves the overall conductivity but also effectively help preserving the structural integrity during cycling. This facile method does not require any cost precursor, harsh reagents, and complicated synthesis processes and can be developed into an eco-friendly way to large-scale production of CTP QDs hybrid materials for applications in energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Lithium-ion batteries
Anode
Coal tar pitch

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52074109 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites.

SEM images of (a) pure Fe3O4, (b) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1 (c) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 (d) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3, (e–g) EDS mapping images based on the image c of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2, (h) TEM image and (i) HRTEM image of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites with different amount of CTP QDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites with different amount of CTP QDs; (b) Fe 2p XPS spectrum, (c) C 1s XPS spectrum and (d) O 1s XPS spectrum of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite.
FT-IR spectra of CTP QDs and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite.
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distributions (inset) for (a) pure Fe3O4, (b) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, (c) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 and (d) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3.
Initial charge/discharge curves of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes in the first cycle at 100 mA g−1.

(a) Rate performance of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes at various current densities; (b) Cycle performance of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes and Coulombic efficiency of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 electrode.
CV curves of (a) pure Fe3O4, (b) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, (c) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 and (d) Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 composite electrodes.
EIS behavior of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes: (a) Nyquist plots; (b) the whole equivalent circuit.
Pore structure parameters and electrical conductivity of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Specific surface area, calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Total pore volume, measured at P/P0 = 0.99.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Micropore volume, estimated from the t-plot method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mesopore volume, calculated by the difference between total pore volume and micropore volume.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Initial charge/discharge capacity and the Coulomb efficiency of CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes.

head.ce:title: A facile method to prepare Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite as advanced anode material for lithium ion batteries

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454003, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) hybrid compositions have aroused wide attention with regard to their application in energy storage; however, complex procedures for their separation, purification and functionalization hinder their practical application. The present study put forward a facile and environmentally friendly coprecipitation approach to prepare Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites using coal tar pitch (CTP) as “natural GQDs” for the first time. When used as anode in lithium ion batteries (LIBs), the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite demonstrates high reversible capacity of 810 mAh g−1 over 200 cycles at the current density of 100 mA g−1. Moreover, at a high current density of 2000 mA g−1, the reversible capacity reaches 547 mAh g−1, which is 6 times that of pure Fe3O4 (91 mAh g−1). The marked ameliorated electrochemical performance is attributed to the fact that the successful coupling between Fe3O4 and CTP QDs can not only improves the overall conductivity but also effectively help preserving the structural integrity during cycling. This facile method does not require any cost precursor, harsh reagents, and complicated synthesis processes and can be developed into an eco-friendly way to large-scale production of CTP QDs hybrid materials for applications in energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with high energy density, high rate capability and reversible capacity as well as long cycle life are critically important for next-generation energy storage system, such as portable electronics, electric vehicles, etc. [1,2]. Worldwide efforts are underway to find novel anode materials with more excellent performance that will be considered as alternative options to replace the currently commercialized graphite anode (372 mAh g−1) [3,4]. With the characteristics of high theoretical capacities (926 mAh g−1), low-cost, environment friendly and natural abundance, Fe3O4 became one of the promising active materials for anode of LIBs [5–7]. However, the cycling stability of capacity for Fe3O4 anode is still unsatisfactory due to the aggregation of Fe3O4 particles and severe volume variation during the charge/discharge cycling process. In order to circumvent the above intractable problems, one efficient strategy is combining Fe3O4 with carbonaceous materials (such as graphene [8,9], carbon nanotubes [10], carbon nanosheets [11], and carbon nano-fibers [12]). It can not only alleviate the inner volume change during the charge/discharge cycling process but also enhance the electronic conductivity of the composites [13–16]. Wang et al. [13] synthesized a bi-morphological Fe3O4@C composite via a chemical vapor deposition method from Fe2O3 nanoparticles by using acetylene as the deposition vapor and carbon source. The Fe3O4@C composite delivers an initial reversible discharge capacity of 570 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1, and the capacity retention after 60 cycles reaches 96%. Kumar et al. [14] synthesized Fe3O4-ONCs@rGO hybrids, which graphite oxide was synthesized by chemical oxidation of natural graphite powder, and was treated with microwave irradiation for exfoliation and reduction into rGO NSs. The synthesized Fe3O4-ONCs@rGO hybrids exhibits outstanding cycling stability (540 mAh g−1 after 120 cycles at 100 mA g−1). Wu et al. [15] prepared double carbon layer 3D network composite anode material of C/Fe3O4/rGO by a two-step method. Firstly, graphene oxide (GO) was used to grow interlayer cauliflower-like Fe3O4 by hydrothermal method. Then, a layer of carbon source was added on the surface of Fe3O4/GO and annealed it to obtain the C/Fe3O4/rGO composite. When used as anode materials for LIBs, the C/Fe3O4/rGO composite exhibits excellent electrochemical performance (844 mAh g−1 at 0.2 A g−1 even after 300 cycles) and high-rate cyclic performance (363 mAh g−1 at 4 A g−1 after 300 cycles). Although great advancements have been made in the development of Fe3O4/carbon, the high cost of those carbon nanomaterials (generally produced by sophisticated chemical vapor deposition, and multistep exfoliation and reduction methods) hinders the commercialization of those Fe3O4-based hybrids. Therefore, it is still a challenge to develop a simple and environmentally method to achieve Fe3O4-based composites.

As emerging carbon nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have uniquely small particle sizes, abundant edge positions and excellent conductivity, thus endowing them with great potential for developing high-performance electrode materials in energy storage [17–19]. Zhu et al. [20]reported a new application in the form of a CQD-based hybrid as an excellent electrode material for supercapacitors. They first employed commercially nanographite powders as the carbon source and prepared CQDs with a chemical oxidation method. The CQDs were then thermally reduced in an argon atmosphere. Finally, the obtained reduced CQD powders (RCQDs) were further combined with hydrous RuO2 via an impregnation method. The RCQD/RuO2 materials demonstrate excellent electrochemical performance with remarkable specific capacitance, ultrahigh rate properties and rather high cycling stability. In LIBs, GQDs can help to relieve the volume expansion, accelerate Li+ diffusion and improve electrochemical properties. Guo et al. [21] prepared GQDs by a hydrothermal method firstly, and then synthesized a composite material of GQDs doped MoS2 nanosheets (GQDs/MoS2) through a series of experimental steps. GQDs/MoS2, the anode material in LIBs, is associated with the advantages such as great cyclic performance, large capacity, as well as remarkable rate capability. Yin et al. [22] designed a hybrid structured NiO@Co3O4@GQDs sphere, which the NiO@Co3O4 with a multilayered hollow structure was prepared using a Co-Ni-based MOF as the precursor firstly, and then GQDs were introduced to NiO@Co3O4 as an external support. The NiO@Co3O4@GQDs exhibits excellent cycling property and a large reversible capacity of 1158 mA h g−1 that remain stable even after 250 cycles at 0.1 A g−1. Zhang et al. [23] prepared C-GQDs via an acid oxidation method using Taixi anthracite powder as the raw material firstly. Then, the antler-shaped α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles were prepared on a nickel substrate in a controllable way via electrodeposition method by adjusting the electrolyte solvent ratio. Finally, the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite material was prepared by two-step electrodeposition with C-GQDs as the electrolyte. When the current density is 1 A g−1, the specific capacitance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 is up to 1582.5 mAh g−1, and it can maintain 1320 mAh g−1 after 110 cycles. However, synthesizing these hybrid electrode materials usually requires preparing GQDs firstly, and then combining it with precursors to form GQDs/X composites, as shown in Table S1. And it also must be pointed out that in the process of preparing GQDs always use a large amount of strong acid or alkali or harsh reaction conditions inevitably, which is not only unfavorable to the environment but also takes a long time to be removed in the subsequent purification process. Therefore, it is necessary to develop more simple and green preparation strategies of GQDs-based composites for anode materials in LIBs. In our previous work, we reported that coal tar pitch (CTP), one of the coking industry by-products, can be regarded as “natural GQDs” because the molecular of CTP is very similar to that of GQDs, and it can directly replace synthetic GQDs to improve the visible light activity of TiO2 [24–26]. Inspired by this perception, we expect to use these kinds of “natural GQDs” to overcome the limitations during preparation of GQDs hybrid compositions for lithium storage.

Herein, we demonstrate a simple and fast approach for the large-scale synthesis of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites, using CTP as “natural GQDs”. The introduction of CTP QDs on the surface of the Fe3O4 not only prevent the aggregation of Fe3O4 but also can provide an efficient medium for Li+ and electron transport, as well as improve the electronic conductivity. As a consequence, compared with the pure Fe3O4, a highly reversible capacity, good rate capability, and excellent cyclic stability are achieved with the composites as anode for lithium storage. This facile synthetic method of Fe3O4@CTP QDs may be further extended to a green and versatile way to synthesis of CTP QDs-based anode materials with excellent lithium storage performance, and more, this approach also explored a high valued-added using way of CTP and reduce its environmental pollution.

Raw CTP was obtained from Henan Zhonghong Coal Chemical Co., Ltd. (Pingdingshan, China), and its light components were driven off after heating at 360 ℃, and then the treated CTP was used as CTP QDs directly during preparation of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites. Ammonia hydroxide (NH3·H2O) were provided by Yantai City Shuangshuang Chemical Reagent Co. Iron dichloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), and tetrahydrofuran (HTF) were offered by Shanghai Macleans Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water (DI) was used throughout the experiment, and all chemical reagents were of analytical grade without further purification.

Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites were prepared via a coprecipitation method and with further carbonization process. In a typical process, 1.165 g FeCl3·6H2O and 1.665 g FeCl2·4H2O were dissolved in 100 mL DI water, and then 0.1 g CTP QDs (dissolved in 20 mL HTF) was added with a vigorous stirring for 20 min. Subsequently, the NH3·H2O was added drop by drop to the mixture until the pH value of mixture reached to 11. Then the mixture was centrifuged to collect the precipitate, and DI water was used to wash the precipitate, followed by overnight drying under 80 ℃. Finally, the dried sample powder was heated to 600 ℃ at 5 ℃ min−1 and kept 2 h in a pure N2 atmosphere. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration of the preparation process of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites. According to the amount of CTP QDs (0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 g) in the precursors, the prepared Fe3O4-based composite samples are referred to as Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2, and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3, respectively. In addition, the pure Fe3O4 was synthesized without CTP QDs using same process.

All the prepared samples were ground to fine powders with an agate mortar. Then, we acquired the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) through the use of Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) under the following conditions, at 40 kV from 5° to 90° at the rate of 10° min−1. Moreover, each sample was characterized by the Raman spectroscopic technique (France). The Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer was utilized to characterize X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) at a 5 × 10−11 Torr pressure. The sample morphologies were evaluated using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6390LV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The pore structure and specific surface area of the samples were investigated by Nitrogen adsorption and desorption on an Autosorb-iQ-MP (Quantachrome, USA) at 77 K, and the sample was degassed at 200 ℃ for 8 h before testing. The electrical conductivity of the samples were measured with a Nanometrics HL5550 cryostat based on the standard four-probe technique at the pressure of 10 MPa.

The two-electrode LIR2016 half-cell was assembled with the working electrode, the counter electrode, and membrane in Ar-filled glove box. The working electrode was obtained as follows. Firstly, the mixture of the prepared samples, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), and acetylene black with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 was added into N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone solvent to obtain a slurry. Subsequently, the prepared compound was deposited on a regularly cut copper foil and dried. Next, the working electrode material was prepared with ∅ = 14 mm and ~2–3 mg of mass, and the counter electrode consisted of a lithium metal sheet that was separated by the membrane of Celgard 2400. Finally, 1 M of LiPF6 solution in ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1, v/v) was employed as the electrolyte. The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests were carried out using a Neware battery testing system (Shenzhen Neware, China) in a voltage ranged from 0.01 to 3.00 V. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured by a CHI760e electrochemical test system (Shanghai Chenhua, China), which CV was performed at a scan rate of 0.5 mV·s−1 and EIS with a frequency range of 0.01–105 Hz. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out at room temperature, and the assembled cells were prepared 24 h prior to the electrochemical tests.

Fig. 2 is the SEM and TEM pictures of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites, showing both pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites are uniform spherical nanoparticles, and the pure Fe3O4 particles are tightly stacked while Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites are composed of dispersed particles, which suggests CTP QDs could restrict aggregation of newly formed Fe3O4. The EDS mapping images of the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite (Fig. 2e–g) demonstrate the uniform distribution of C, Fe, and O elements in the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite, indicating the existence of CTP QDs in Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite. The TEM image (Fig. 2h) shows that the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite is stacked with uniformly sized nanosheets. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2i) of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 confirms that there are two phases that the interplanar spacing of lattice fringes marked in the right part is 0.21 nm are assigned to the theoretical interplane spacing for graphite of the (100) plane [25], while the left part one is 0.25 nm representing (311) plane of Fe3O4 [27–29].

The XRD patterns of the CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites as well as the standard XRD pattern of cubic Fe3O4 (JCPDS card no. 19–0629) are provided in Fig. 3a [30,31]. The CTP QDs displays a weak broad peak around 2θ = 26.6 ℃, indicating the stacking of the aromatic layer structure in CTP QDs [25]. All diffraction peaks can be indexed to the standard single phase cubic Fe3O4 structure, no characteristic diffraction signal from CTP QDs is detected in all Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites, which may be attributed to the small amount, high dispersion and low crystallinity of CTP QDs in the composites [25].

Fig. 3b displays the Raman spectra of CTP QDs and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites. The two characteristic peaks at about 1343 and 1582 cm−1 are observed in both CTP QDs and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites, corresponding to carbon materials structural disorder band (D band) and in-plane sp2 vibration crystalline band (G band), respectively [32,33], which demonstrates the existence of CTP QDs in Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites. In addition, it can detected that the Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites have a small peak at 670 cm−1, which corresponds to the typical A1 g model vibration of Fe3O4 [34], which proves that Fe3O4 exists in the composite material.

XPS technique was carried out to recognize the surface composition and element valence of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites ( Fig. 4). As shown in Fig. 4a, the survey XPS spectra of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites display strong signals of iron (Fe 2p1/2, 2p3/2), oxygen (O 1s) and carbon (C 1s). The Fe 2p1/2, 2p3/2 and O 1s signals are derived from Fe3O4, while C 1s signal comes from CTP QDs, which proves CTP QDs are successful combined with Fe3O4. High-resolution Fe 2p spectrum depicted that two broad peaks at 724.8 eV and 710.9 eV (Fig. 4b), are related to Fe 2p1/2 and 2p3/2, which are in agreement with previously reported values for Fe3O4 [34]. Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 can be divided into two parts due to the presence of dual oxidation states including Fe2+ and Fe3+ species in Fe3O4, which the signals at 722.5 eV and 708.5 eV could be corresponded to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 of Fe2+ species, and the signals at 724.9 eV and 712.5 eV could be attributed to Fe 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 in Fe3+, respectively. The peak of C 1s contains two components: the main peak located at binding energy ~284.5 eV corresponded to graphite carbon and a small peak at 285.4 eV can be fitted to C-O [35]. Fig. 4d shows the surface O 1s signal of materials, which is deconvoluted into two parts. Among them, Fe-O occurs at the binding energy ~529.8 eV [36]. It is worth noting that FeOC linkage appears at 531.5 eV [36]. Such a bond could be considered as the consequence of in-situ redox reaction on the surface during the carbonization process. More importantly, FeOC linkage is strongly believed to make crucial contribution to hold a complete and stable structure.

Fig. 5 displays the FT-IR spectra of CTP QDs and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite. The FT-IR spectrum of CTP QDs displays the existence of aromatic H-Csp2 (740 cm−1), CO (1026 cm−1), CC (1500 cm−1), CC (1601 cm−1), and OH (3420 cm−1) vibrations [25]. Compared to GQDs, the emerging peak at 573 cm−1 in Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite is corresponding to the characteristic signal vibration of the FeO bond in Fe3O4 [34]. In addition, in the spectrum of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 composite, we believe that CO, CC, and CC bands are derived from CTP QDs, and OH is designated to the vibrations of surface adsorbed water/OH. The FT-IR results are in accordance with the XPS results, and the presence of CTP QDs in the composite is further proved.

Fig. 6 presents the porous features and pore sizes distribution of both Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites and pure Fe3O4 samples analyzed through nitrogen adsorption/desorption. According to Fig. 6, the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms show a hysteresis loop, implying the existence of mesopores in all samples [37]. Pore distribution curves (inset of Fig. 6) exhibit that the pore sizes of pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites are mainly distributed in a range of 2–40 nm or larger. The specific surface area of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 are calculated to be 57, 98 and 180 m2 g−1, respectively, which larger than that of pure Fe3O4 (16 m2 g−1) as listed in Table 1. Notably, Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites exhibit enhanced electrical conductivity values with the increasing of CTP QDs addition (Table 1), especially for Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 (248.6 S m−1). The appropriate specific surface area, narrow pore size distribution and superior electrical conductivity of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites make them promising electrode materials used for high-performance LIBs.

The charge/discharge curves of the CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes at a current density of 100 mA g−1 were investigated by GCD. As shown in Figs. 7 and S1, CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 electrodes deliver a discharge specific capacity of 344, 1036, 1184, 1701 and 1335 mAh g−1, and the corresponding initial Coulombic efficiency are 72.67%, 56.85%, 64.95%, 60.00% and 59.70%, respectively, as shown in Table 2. The composite electrodes display a higher lithium-storage property and an obvious initial Coulombic efficiency than the pure Fe3O4 electrode thanks to the introduction of CTP QDs in the composites, which not only can improve the overall conductivity of the electrode material, but also can effectively buffer the volume expansion during charging and discharging [38–40]. More importantly, with the increase of the amount of CTP QDs, the charge/discharge specific capacity and initial Coulombic efficiency of Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 electrode begins to decrease, which may be related to its larger specific surface area and pore volume, which is consistent with the pore structure analysis results shown in Table 1.

From Fig. 8a, it can be seen that the rate properties from 100 to 2000 mA g−1 were characterized to further investigate the electrochemical performance. The specific capacity of electrode materials decreases with increasing the current density. The specific capacities of pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 electrodes at 2000 mA g−1 are 91, 300, 547 and 381 mAh g−1, respectively. After several charge/discharge process under large currents, the capacity of each sample can still go back to 410, 633, 860 and 727 mAh g−1 when the current is reduced to 100 mA g−1. Obviously, the capacity order with different current densities is represented by Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 > Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 > Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1 > pure Fe3O4. Furthermore, the rate performance of CTP QDs is shown in Fig. S2. Here, the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 electrode exhibits the highest capacities at all current densities, and the ratio of Fe3O4 to CTP QDs is one of the key factor for the electrochemical performances. These data indicate that the introduction of CTP QDs can significantly improve the rate performance of the Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes. In particularly, the return to a small-current charge and discharge after large-current charge and discharge restores the capacities of the composite materials to a good level. We conclude that the excellent electrical conductivity of CTP QDs effectively improves the transfer rate and stability of electrons on the surface of the composite materials-this improves the rate performance of the composite material [41].

The cycling profiles of each electrode sample and the Coulombic efficiency of the Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 electrode are shown in Figs. 8b and S3. After 200 cycles at 100 mA g−1, the discharge capacities of CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-1, Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs-3 are 108, 335, 608, 810, and 678 mAh g−1, respectively. The Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 electrode exhibits the best stable cyclability and the highest capacity, moreover, the Coulombic efficiency remains ~99.0% during the whole cycling process except the beginning cycles. Table S2. summarizes the as-prepared Fe3O4@CTP QDs-2 shows better cycling performance compared to other reported composites.

Fig. S4 and Fig. 9 show the results of CV curves for CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes at the initial three cycles in the voltage range from 0.01 to 3.0 V at a scanning rate of 0.5 mV s−1, respectively. In the CV curves of CTP QDs (Fig. S4), showing typical CV curves of carbonaceous anode materials. A pronounced reduction peak located at around 0.75 V is observed in the first cycle, and this peak is not obvious in the subsequent cycles. This is generally attributed to the decomposition of the electrolyte during initial Li+ intercalation and the formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film [42–44]. Besides that, another sharp reduction peak is also observed at around 0 V, which is attributed to the reversible insertion of Li+ into the graphitic carbons [45]. In the deintercalation of Li+ process, the weak oxidation peaks at around 0.2 V is associated with the extraction of Li+ from the graphitic carbons [46]. For pure Fe3O4, as shown in Fig. 9a, a reduction peak is observed at ~0.27 V in the first cathodic process, which can be attributed to the reduction of Fe3+/Fe2+ to Fe0. However, this peak will shift to higher voltage ~0.70 V in the subsequent cycles, indicating that structural changes occur in the Fe3O4 nanoparticles after the Li+ insertion in the first cycle. And decrease of the peak intensity indicates the irreversible Li+ loss due to the SEI formation during the first cycle. In the anodic process, a peak is recorded at about ~1.75 V, corresponding to the oxidation of Fe0 to Fe2+/Fe3+ [47], and their integrated area decreased with cycling. On the basis of the reactions, the whole electrochemical process can be expressed as follows [48]:
(1) Fe3O4 + xLi+ + xe− ⟶ Lix(Fe3O4)
(2) Lix(Fe3O4) + (8−x)Li+ +(8−x)e− ⟶ 3Fe0+4Li2O
(3) 3Fe0 + 4Li2O ⟶ Fe3O4 + 8Li++8e−

For the Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites, the CV curves are similar to those of pure Fe3O4. In the first cycle, their reduction and the oxidation peaks are similar at around ~0.30 V and ~1.75 V, respectively [49]. They are positively shifted during subsequent cycles due to less polarization of the electrode materials compared with the first cycle. The CV curves after the first cycle almost overlapped, indicating the good electrochemical reversibility of Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites.

The kinetics process of CTP QDs, pure Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes during the operation of the batteries may be revealed by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis and their Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. 10a. The Nyquist plots of the samples consist of three parts, low, medium and high frequency region [50]. According to high frequency region, the diameter of the semicircle closely refers to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) [51]. In medium frequency region, the region is related to the 45° slash named as Warburg impedance [52]. Furthermore, the low frequency region stands for the ion diffusion resistance in the electrolyte. The model of the electrical equivalent circuit is depicted in the Fig. 10b, where Rs, Rct and Re present the electrolyte resistance, charge transfer resistance and intrinsic resistance of electrodes, and Wi denotes the Warburg impedance. Apparently, the diameter of the semicircles for Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes in the high frequency region is significantly smaller than that of pure Fe3O4, indicating that the Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes display smaller charge transfer resistance. This means that the electrode reaction can process more effectively in Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes due to the introduction of CTP QDs, which can enhance the electronic conductivity. The enhanced electrode reaction kinetics leads to a better electrochemical performance of the Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite electrodes.

In conclusion, when coal tar pitch is directly used to be the GQDs natural alternative, an excellent Fe3O4@CTP QDs composite materials can be synthesized successfully through a facile and green one-step coprecipitation approach, without harsh reagents/conditions, tedious post-treatment purification procedures. The characterization results indicate that Fe3O4 and CTP QDs can be tightly coupled together by forming Fe-O-C. The obtained Fe3O4@CTP QDs composites manifest distinct enhanced electrochemical lithium storage properties than pristine Fe3O4, such as high reversible capacity, good cyclic stability, as well as excellent rate performance. These could be assigned to that the introduction of CTP QDs will improve the electrical conductivity and thus facilitate the charge transfer within the electrode; and more, CTP QDs enhance the chemical and physical structure stability of Fe3O4 and buffer the volume variation during the charge and discharge. The current research provides a facile and versatile way for large-scale production of CTP QDs hybrid materials for energy storage.

Feng Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Quanrun Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Zhanzhan Jin: Resources, Investigation, Software. Guangxu Huang: Resources. Baolin Xing: Resources. Jianbo Jia: Resources. Chuanxiang Zhang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074109).
